content.assessment: Migrated lens reading for 'Admissions and intake-equity regulation (keep structure, fix the gate)'. Hungary cannot feasibly implement a structural reform eliminating or significantly delaying early selection before 2030 without (a) substantial prior teacher-workforce stabilization in rural and STEM areas, (b) explicit sequencing away from concurrent National Core Curriculum and AI-integration reforms, and (c) multi-party legislative consensus to protect against political reversal—only the first precondition is currently in progress.
opportunities: Reduced SES-skew in admitted cohorts at the 6/8-year gate, addressing the documented near-zero intake share of multiply-disadvantaged pupils.
Preserves the approximately 10% competency-gain advantage associated with 6/8-year tracks for admitted students, since the track structure itself is untouched.
strengths: Hungary's teaching workforce is structurally aging and contracting: approximately 50% of full-time primary teachers are age 50 or older, only 7.2% are under 30, and total teacher numbers have declined by over 21,000 since 2003/2004 (164,000 in 2023/2024 vs. 185,000+ in 2003/2004).

Teacher shortages in mathematics and natural sciences are acute; fewer than 3,400 people applied for university pedagogical degrees in 2024, with history–English combinations dominating demand for other subjects.
threats: Implementation by ministerial decree rather than cardinal law leaves the mechanism exposed to reversal by a subsequent government within a single electoral cycle; the scenario does not propose a specific entrenchment design (e.g., cardinal-law status, cross-party pact, or sunset-clause safeguard) to mitigate this.
Fidesz's demonstrated framing of any selection-related change as ideologically totalitarian creates reversal risk even for a narrower admissions-only intervention, not only for structural abolition.
weaknesses: The actual magnitude of net positive equity gain in Hungary from delayed selection (holding school-choice and catchment rules constant) remains unknown; Polish gains may not generalize given Hungary's significantly higher baseline segregation and denser network of small rural schools.

The cost (in transition staffing, retraining, and administrative disruption) of a full or partial phase-out of early selection in Hungary is unquantified; Polish and other OECD countries' transition budgets are not published in comparable form.
